segments.s315-g137: اگر چند here functions like the concessive اگرچه — “even if” / “though.” اندیشه is the subject (“thought” / “deliberation”), گردد is the classical present/subjunctive form of گشت used as “becomes” or “may become,” and دراز is a postpositive predicate adjective “long.” The whole clause means “even if much thought becomes long” = “though the deliberation may be prolonged.”
segments.s315-g138: هم here means “even / still,” linking back to the concessive. از + پاک یزدان supplies the source/relation (بی‌نیاز normally takes از: “free from / independent of”), and نه‌ای is an archaic negative copula (roughly “is not” / “there is not”). بی‌نیاز is the predicate “free / without need.” Word order is emphatic/classical: “even from the Pure God (پاک یزدان) (there) is not (نه‌ای) [one] free (بی‌نیاز).”
segments.s315-g139: به یک ماه is a temporal phrase meaning “within/in the space of one month”; به here marks that temporal extent. در marks the locative “in,” and آذرآبادگان is the place name (Azarābāydgān/Azerbaijan). The sequence gives time then place: “in one month, in Azarābāydgān.”
segments.s315-g140: ببودند is an archaic plural past/existential verb meaning “there were” / “they were” (the b‑prefix is classical). It is followed by the plural subjects شاهان و آزادگان (“kings and the free/nobles”). The verb asserts the presence or existence of those groups in the stated time/place.
segments.s315-g121: نشستند is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person plural verb 'they sat/mounted'; in epic Persian نشستن is commonly used for mounting a horse. با + باژ here means 'with a cry/ shout' (با = 'with', باژ = 'cry/noise'). هر دو ('each two') is the fixed phrase meaning 'both'. بر اسب follows the verb and marks where/what they mounted: 'upon horse(s)'. Word order is subject‑verb‑adjunct typical of Persian narrative; the phrase groups as 'they mounted on horses with a cry, both'.
segments.s315-g122: دوان (here a verbal adverb/participial sense) means 'galloped/ran quickly' and modifies the preceding action; it is not a finite verb here but describes manner. تا سوی = 'toward' or 'as far as toward' — سوی is the preposition 'toward(s), to' and خان is the title 'khan' (lord); آذرگشسب is the proper name of the hero. The whole segment expresses direction/purpose: they galloped toward Khan Azar‑Gashasp.
segments.s315-g123: پر از بیم دل is an adjectival phrase 'full (پر) of fear (بیم) of heart (دل)'; Persian uses the order 'پر از X دل' to mean 'their heart full of X'. یک به یک is an idiom meaning 'one by one' or 'one after another' (یک = 'one', به here functions adverbially 'by/one‑by‑one'). پرامید is a predicate adjective 'hopeful'; syntactically this sequence describes the state of their hearts changing 'from fear to hope, one by one.'
segments.s315-g124: برفتند is the past, 3rd‑person plural 'they went/departed' (بر‑ often prefixed in older Persian verbs). با introduces the manner/accompaniment 'with'; جامه‌های سپید is the noun phrase 'white garments' (جامه‌های = plural 'garments', سپید = 'white'). The clause states how they left: 'they departed with white garments.'
segments.s315-g125: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. آتش بدیدند is a simple past clause 'they saw fire' (بدیدند = 'they saw'). گریان شد(ند) is a predicative compound: گریان 'weeping' plus شدند 'they became'—together 'they wept'/'they became weeping'. The sequence gives a temporal cause–effect: upon seeing the fire, they became weeping.
segments.s315-g126: The second چو again means 'when'. بر آتش = 'upon the fire'; تیز 'sharp/keen/fierce' modifies آتش; بریان شد(ند) is passive/subjective 'they were roasted' or 'became roasted' (بریان = 'roasted', شد(ند) = 'became/they were'). The clause describes what happened to them when exposed to the fierce fire: they were roasted quickly.
segments.s315-g127: بدان جایگه is an archaic locative phrase meaning 'in/at that place' (ب here is the prepositional prefix 'to/in', and دان combines in older usage to form 'بدان' = 'to/at that'). جایگه = 'place/spot'; زار و گریان are predicative adjectives 'wretched and weeping'; دو شاه = 'the two kings'. Word order places the locative first, then the descriptive state and the subject.
segments.s315-g128: ببودند is an archaic/periphrastic past plural 'they were' (ب‑ + بودند). با indicates accompaniment/condition 'with'; درد و فریاد = 'pain and outcry/crying' describing their state. خواه at the end is difficult/archaic here; it can be an auxiliary or emphatic particle but does not add a new tense — the clause means roughly 'they were (found) with pain and cries.'
segments.s315-g57: بدو گفت شاه این ز بخت تو بود is a compact reported-speech clause: بدو (to him) is an indirect object prepositional/adverbial element 'to him'; گفت شاه is the past-tense main verb cluster 'the king said'; این ز بخت تو بود is the content of what was said, with این 'this' as the demonstrative subject, ز بخت 'from/ because of fortune' giving the causal phrase, تو 'you', and بود the past copula 'was'. Word order follows classical/poetic Persian where the copula appears at the end.
segments.s315-g58: برومند شاخ درخت تو بود is a nominal predicate describing the addressee: برومند 'luxuriant/robust' qualifies شاخ درخت 'the bough/branch of the tree', تو is the possessor 'your', and بود is the past copula 'was' — together meaning 'you were the flourishing bough of the tree.' The adjective precedes the head noun in this poetic word order.
segments.s315-g59: زبرجد بیاورد و یاقوت و زر lists treasures: زبرجد (a gem) is the object of the verb بیاورد 'he brought'; the conjunctions و simply join یاقوت (ruby) and زر (gold). The verb بیاورد is a past/epic narrative form 'brought/produced' placed after its first object for poetic effect.
segments.s315-g60: همی ریخت بر تارک شاه بر is a descriptive participial/clausal continuation: همی (an iterative marker) + ریخت (past of ریختن 'to pour/rain') = 'kept pouring' or 'were poured continually'; بر تارک شاه 'upon the king's crown/head' is the prepositional phrase indicating target. Note تارک here is used poetically for the crown/top of the head; the final بر repeats the preposition for metrical/poetic balance.
segments.s315-g61: بدین گونه تا تخت گوهرنگار is an adverbial temporal/manner phrase: بدین گونه 'in this manner/thus' (here ب functions as the locative/prefixal part of the demonstrative), تا 'until', followed by تخت گوهرنگار 'the gem-studded throne' as the goal/timepoint reached. The phrase sets up a temporal span leading to what follows.
segments.s315-g62: بشد پایه‌ها ناپدید از نثار contains the result clause: بشد (ب + شد) here is the narrative past 'it became/was made', پایه‌ها 'the pedestals/bases' is the noun in subject position, ناپدید 'vanished/was concealed' describes their state, and از نثار 'by/from the offerings' gives the cause or means. The order places the verb بشد early (poetic inversion) with the resulting adjective-clause following.
segments.s315-g63: بفرمود پس کانجمن را بخوان is an order clause: بفرمود (honorific past 'he commanded/said') introduces the speech; پس 'then'; کانجمن را 'the assembly' marked with را as definite object; بخوان is an imperative infinitival form meaning here 'summon/call together' (poetic uses of بخوان can mean 'call' as well as 'read'). The verb is the command directed at attendants.
segments.s315-g64: به ایوان دیگر بیارای خوان is the continuation of the command specifying where and what to prepare: به ایوان دیگر 'to the other iwan' (prepositional phrase), بیارای (here a verb meaning 'prepare/adorn/arrange') governs خوان 'the banquet/table'. The verb + noun form instructs attendants to set up the feast in another hall; word order places destination before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s315-g97: چنین (an adverb) comes first for emphasis: “thus/so.” گفت is the past 3rd‑person singular verb “said”; in this poetic line the verb appears before the subject (inverted word order). خسرو is the subject (the speaker), and به + کاووس شاه is the indirect object/dative: “to Kavus, the king” (شاه is an appositive/title after the name).
segments.s315-g98: جز از کردگار is a negative/exclusive phrase “except/from God.” The following clause از که جوییم راه is a rhetorical question: که = “whom” and جوییم is 1st‑person plural present (here deliberative/subjunctive sense) “shall we seek/should we seek.” راه is the object “way/method.” Note the archaic repetition of از and the compact, poetic ordering.
segments.s315-g99: A stock list of places joined by و: بیابان “desert,” یک‌ساله modifies دریا as a compound adjective “one‑year” or “year‑long” sea, and کوه “mountain.” The adjective precedes the noun in this compressed poetic sequence.
segments.s315-g100: برفتیم is the past 1st‑person plural “we went/advanced” (بر‑ adds the sense of setting forth). با introduces an accompanying/instrumental phrase: با داغ دل = “with hearts burning” (داغ دل = grief/heartache). یک گروه is the noun phrase identifying the travelling company; the verb precedes the noun phrase for concision and poetic rhythm.
segments.s315-g101: A list of locations introduced by به: به هامون و کوه و به دریای آب = “to the plain and mountain and to the sea of water.” دریای آب is a poetic doubled expression (“sea’s water”) for emphasis; repetition of به is stylistic and rhythmic.
segments.s315-g102: نشانی ندیدیم is a negated past 1st‑person plural clause: “we did not see a trace/sign.” ز here is the poetic form of از meaning “of/from,” introducing the source: ز افراسیاب = “of Afrasiab” (i.e., any sign belonging to/coming from him).
segments.s315-g103: گر is the conditional “if.” او is the subject “he.” یک زمان is a temporal phrase “for one moment/at any time.” اندر آید = اندر (archaic “in/into”) + آید (3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive “comes”) rendering a conditional “if he were to come in/enter.” به گنگ is a prepositional phrase modifying آید; گنگ is an adjective/word here used adverbially (poetic/archaic usage).
segments.s315-g104: سپاه آرد uses سپاه (army) as the subject and آرد (archaic 3rd‑person singular present of آوردن) “brings” as the verb; this is a simple result clause following the condition. از هر سویی = “from every direction/side,” and بیدرنگ is an adverb “immediately/at once,” placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s315-g89: به اندازه‌شان is a prepositional phrase meaning “according to their measure/standing”; اندازه with the enclitic -شان is ‘their rank/degree’. خلعت is the direct object ‘robe(s) of honour’. آراستند is the literary past, 3rd-person plural of آراستن (آرای‑), ‘they adorned/bestowed’. Word order here places the measure phrase before the object and verb (typical poetic inversion); the verb agrees with an implicit plural subject (‘they’).
segments.s315-g90: ز گنج is a prepositional phrase ‘from the treasury’. آنچه begins a free relative clause ‘that which/whatever’, modified by the comparative adjective پرمایه‌تر (‘more substantial’; پرمایه + -تر). خواستند is past 3rd-person plural ‘they wanted/desired’. Together the clause means ‘they took/allowed whatever was more valuable that they wished’.
segments.s315-g91: برفتند is the past 3rd-person plural of رفتن with the prefix بر- (poetic/literary) meaning ‘they departed/they went’. هر کس (‘each person’) is the distributive subject phrase; سوی کشوری is the directional complement ‘toward a country/land’. The verb is fronted for stylistic effect, with the noun phrase following it.
segments.s315-g92: سرافراز is a predicative/adjectival description ‘proud/triumphant’. با introduces accompaniment (‘with’), and نامور لشکری is a noun phrase ‘a famed/renowned army/host’ (نامور = celebrated; لشکری = troops/soldiery). The whole phrase describes the manner/accompaniment of their departure.
segments.s315-g93: بپرداخت is the past form meaning ‘he/she/they paid’ (here financial payment). زآن پس (‘from that, then/after that’) is a temporal phrase signalling continuation. به کار سپاه is the prepositional object ‘for the army’s work/affairs’ (به کار = for the business of). The subject is omitted and understood from context (the king or treasury officials).
segments.s315-g94: درم داد literally ‘gave dirhams’ (درم = coin; داد = gave, past 3rd sg/pl depending on subject). یک‌ساله here means ‘one‑year (sum)’ i.e. ‘a year’s worth’ and qualifies the amount given. از گنج شاه is the source phrase ‘from the king’s treasury’. Word order: object/amount before the source phrase and after the verb, as is common in Persian.
segments.s315-g95: وزآن پس is a compound temporal connector ‘and from that/and thereafter’. نشستند is the past 3rd-person plural ‘they sat down’ (here the correct meaning is ‘sat’, but the provided lemma senses did not include ‘sit’ so the sense is marked -1). بی‌انجمن is an adverbial/phrase meaning ‘without an assembly/council’ (i.e. sitting apart from formal council).
segments.s315-g96: نیا و جهانجوی is a coordinated noun phrase ‘the elders/ancients (نیا) and the world-seekers/ambitious (جهانجوی)’. و is the conjunction ‘and’. با introduces accompaniment ‘with’, and رای‌زن means ‘adviser(s)/counselor(s)’. The whole phrase identifies who sat and with whom (their advisers).
segments.s315-g25: به ایوانها (prepositional phrase) sets the location ('into/at the porticoes') before the object; تخت زرین is the direct object with the adjective زرین post‑nominal as is normal in Persian. نهاد is the simple past (third person plural implied) of نهادن 'to place/put', so the clause reads 'they placed a golden throne in the iwans.'
segments.s315-g26: به خانه در is a locative phrase where در here means 'door/gate' (i.e. 'at the house-doors'); آرایش چین is a noun phrase ('an ornament/arrangement of folds' — a pleated decoration) that precedes the verb. نهاد is again past tense 'placed/put' (they put/hung). The word order (locative + object + verb) is a typical literary inversion.
segments.s315-g27: ببستند is the third‑person plural past form meaning 'they tied/fastened' (literary/compound form of بستن). آذین is the object 'decoration(s)'. The following prepositional phrases به شهر و به راه mark the places where the decorations were hung. Verb + object + locative sequence is a normal literary word order.
segments.s315-g28: همه here functions as an adverb/quantifier 'everywhere/all', modifying the following list of places. The nouns برزن, کوی, بازارگاه are coordinated (and … and …) to give a full list of public places; there is no overt verb in this fragment because it attaches to the previous clause's action.
segments.s315-g29: پذیره is a noun meaning 'reception' or 'welcome' used here with a verbal expression; شدندش is a composite where شدند is past 'they became' and the enclitic -ش = 'him/it' marks the person received. Read together as a literary/periphrastic passive/recipient expression: 'he was received' / 'they received him.' همه مهتران is the subject/group doing the reception ('all the chiefs/elders').
segments.s315-g30: بزرگان هر شهر is a genitive phrase 'the great ones of each city' (بزرگان = headmen/great men; هر شهر follows to mean 'of every city'). The coordinating و links کنداوران as another group. This is a simple nominal coordination apposed to the preceding 'همه مهتران'.
segments.s315-g31: همه راه و بی‌راه is an adverbial expression meaning 'every road and byroad' (i.e. 'everywhere'); it precedes the verbless adjectival phrase گنبد زده. گنبد زده is a compound where گنبد (dome) + زده (past participle 'struck/affixed') functions adjectivally — 'dotted/adorned with domes' — so the whole fragment means 'every road and byroad was (or became) dotted with domes.'
segments.s315-g32: جهان شد is 'the world became' (شد = past of شدن 'to become'). چو here means 'like/as' and introduces the comparison 'like brocade (دیبا)'. به زر آزده is a short prepositional phrase where به indicates the instrumental/ornamental sense 'with/by' and زر names the gold; آزده is used poetically to mean 'adorned/tinged' (archaic), so the clause reads 'the world became like brocade, adorned with gold.'
segments.s315-g113: This chunk strings together a small prepositional/exclamatory phrase: 'ابا' functions here as an exclamatory/particle-like item (uncertain/archaic) placed before the following noun group; 'باژ' is a noun meaning 'cry' and stands next to the preposition 'با' ('with') that governs the following noun phrase 'کردگار جهان' ('the Maker of the world'). Word order is poetically compact: [particle] + [instrument/cry] + با + [object/God].
segments.s315-g114: 'بدو' is a dative/adverbial phrase meaning 'to him' (i.e. toward God). 'بر کنیم' is a light/verbal construction where 'بر' (here 'to/toward') combines with the present-subjunctive/optative 'کنیم' ('we do/make') to mean 'we offer/raise' — the object of that action follows as 'آفرین' ('acclamation/praise'). 'نهان' is an adverb/adjective placed after the verb phrase giving the manner 'secretly' or 'hiddenly'. The sequence therefore reads: to him / (toward) we offer / praise / secretly.
segments.s315-g115: 'بباشیم' is a hortative/subjunctive first-person-plural form meaning 'let us be/let us stand' (an older/poetic imperative). It is followed by the locative phrase 'بر پیش آتش' ('before/in the presence of the fire') and the manner phrase 'به پای' ('on foot'); together they describe the place and manner of the action: 'let us stand before the fire on foot.'
segments.s315-g116: 'مگر' here introduces a conditional/exceptive clause meaning 'unless' or 'if only.' The phrase 'پاک یزدان' ('pure God') is the subject, followed by the past copula 'بود' ('was') and the predicate noun 'رهنمای' ('guide'). The clause thus reads literally 'unless the pure God was guide,' i.e. 'unless the pure God were our guide.' Note the past copula is used in a conditional/hypothetical sense.
segments.s315-g117: 'به جایی که' begins a prepositional phrase with a relative clause: 'to a place that/where...' The relative clause 'او دارد آرامگاه' places the verb 'دارد' ('has') after the subject 'او' ('he'), with 'آرامگاه' ('tomb/resting place') as its object: 'to the place where he has a tomb.'
segments.s315-g118: This clause reports the action of a messenger: 'نماید' is an archaic/present/historic-present form of the verb 'to show' placed before the noun phrase; the following words 'نماینده داد راه' place the agent 'نماینده' ('envoy/representative') before the past verb 'داد' ('gave') and its object 'راه' ('way/path'). The whole line thus says that the envoy showed/gave the way to that place.
segments.s315-g119: 'بر این باژ' is a prepositional phrase 'at/upon this cry' (literally 'upon this cry'). The verb 'گشتند' is past plural 'they turned/turned toward,' and 'هر دو یکی' is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'both (together) as one.' Word order is typical poetic inversion: locative phrase → verb → emphatic subject phrase.
segments.s315-g120: The negative/result clause says nothing came from the road: 'نگردید' is a past negative/historical form meaning 'did not appear/occur' (here used impersonally), followed by 'یک تن' ('one person' — i.e. not a single person), 'ز' (poetic 'from'), 'راه' ('the road'), and 'اندکی' ('a little' / 'even a short distance'). Together they mean that not a single person came even a little way from the road.
segments.s315-g33: همه مشک با گوهر آمیختند is a past plural clause: همه مشک ('all the musk') is the noun phrase, با گوهر is a comitative/prepositional phrase 'with jewels' that follows the noun, and آمیختند is a past plural verb 'they mixed/mingled'. The plural verb is used poetically for an inanimate substance or collective action; the agent is unspecified.
segments.s315-g34: ز گنبد به سرها فرو ریختند is a directional clause: ز گنبد 'from the dome' (ز = 'from'), به سرها 'toward/onto the heads' (به gives direction), فرو is an adverbial element 'down(ward)' placed before the verb for emphasis, and ریختند is the past plural 'they poured/streamed down'. The plural verb links back to the previous plural predicate, so the two clauses form a single event.
segments.s315-g35: چو بیرون شد از شهر کاووس کی is a temporal subordinate: چو + past verb (چو = 'when') gives 'when [he] went out'. بیرون شد literally 'became outside' (an idiomatic way to say 'went out'). از شهر کاووس is the source phrase 'out of the city of Kavus'. The final کی here is part of a proper-name construction (the title element Ki-/Kay) that attaches to the following name.
segments.s315-g36: ابا نامداران فرخنده‌پی is a name + epithets: ابا (Āba) is the personal name (here following the title element Ki-), نامداران is an adjective/nickname 'renowned' in plural/formal adjective use, and فرخنده‌پی is an epithet 'fortunate-footed'. Together they form a single proper name phrase in apposition to the subject of the temporal clause.
segments.s315-g37: سوی طالقان آمد و مرو رود is two coordinated pieces: سوی طالقان آمد 'came toward Taleqan' (سوی + place = 'toward'), and the following phrase مرو رود is a compound noun 'the Merv River' (مرو qualifying رود). The و connects the motion clause to the ensuing description about the river.
segments.s315-g38: جهان بود پر بانگ و آوای رود is a predicative clause: جهان بود پر 'the world was full' (بود = past 'was', پر = 'full of'), followed by the contents 'بانگ و آوای رود' — 'the cry and the voice of the river'. آوای رود is a genitive-like construction 'voice of the river' (poetic word order; the possessive relation is expressed without an explicit ezāfe).
segments.s315-g39: و زآن پس به راه نشاپور شاه is an adverbial/fronted phrase setting time and direction: و زآن پس 'and after that', به راه نشاپور 'on the road to Nishapur' (به راه + place), with شاه following as the subject 'the king'. The order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: the destination phrase precedes the subject.
segments.s315-g40: بدیدند مر یکدگر را به راه is a past plural clause: بدیدند 'they saw' is the main verb, یکدگر را is the object 'one another' (را is the definite/object marker), and به راه 'on the road' indicates location. The particle مر here is an archaic/poetic clitic-like element appearing with reciprocal meaning or emphasis; the plural verb marks that multiple people saw each other.
segments.s315-g81: بیاراست is a literary/past form meaning 'he adorned' (3rd person singular); in this poetic line the verb appears before its object phrase. آن گلشن زرنگار is the object phrase: آن 'that/the' + گلشن 'garden' with زرنگار as a postnominal adjective/compound meaning 'gold‑adorned' or 'gilded'.
segments.s315-g82: می‌آورد is the imperfective/presentive form (mi‑prefix + stem) used narratively for an ongoing or habitual action ('would bring' / 'brought/was bringing') in 3rd singular. یاقوت‌لب میگسار is the object/noun phrase 'ruby‑lipped cupbearer' (یاقوت‌لب as adjectival epithet modifying میگسار). Word order here places the verb before the nominal phrase.
segments.s315-g83: به یک هفته is a temporal phrase meaning 'after one week' (literary usage of به marking time); ز ایوان کاووس is a prepositional phrase 'from the iwan of Kavus' where ز governs the following genitive; کی is the archaic/interrogative/temporal particle here meaning 'when' and introduces the temporal clause.
segments.s315-g84: همی is an old narrative/imperfective marker often giving a continuing or habitual sense ('then/was'), placed before the verb. موج is the subject 'a wave' and برخاست is the past/preterite 'rose/arose' (3rd singular). از جام می is a prepositional phrase 'from the cup of wine' (از + جام 'cup' + می 'wine' as possessor/content).
segments.s315-g85: به هشتم is a temporal phrase 'on the eighth (day)' (به used here to mark time). در گنج here means 'the door/gate of the treasury' (در as 'door/gate' + گنج 'treasury/vault'), بگشاد is the past form 'he opened' (literary), and شاه is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb as is common in poetic word order.
segments.s315-g86: همی again gives a narrative/continuing tone. ساخت is the past of 'to build/make' (he made/established). آن رنج را پایگاه: آن 'that', رنج 'the toil/hardship/effort' is marked by را (definite object marker), and پایگاه is the complement 'a base/headquarters' — together 'he built/established a base for that toil.'
segments.s315-g87: بزرگان is the plural noun 'the great ones/nobles'. که بودند forms a relative clause 'who were' (که introducing the clause; بودند = past plural of 'to be'). با او به هم is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'with him, together' (با او 'with him'; به هم = 'together'). Word order places the relative clause after بزرگان as in Persian.
segments.s315-g88: This is a series of prepositional phrases introduced by به meaning roughly 'in/at/for' (here marking the spheres or occasions): 'به رزم' (in battle), 'به بزم' (in banquet/feast), 'به شادی' (in joy) and 'غم' paired by و 'and' to give 'and in sorrow'. The repetition of به is a poetic parallelism listing contexts in which they accompanied him.
segments.s315-g129: جهان‌آفرین را — جهان‌آفرین is the definite object ('the Creator, maker of the world'); را is the Persian definite/object marker attaching to that noun. همی + خواندند is a literary/progressive past construction: همی here gives a continuing or habitual sense ('kept on' / 'were continually'), and خواندند is the 3rd-person plural past, so the phrase means 'they repeatedly invoked/called on the Creator.'
segments.s315-g130: بدان موبدان گوهر افشاندند — here بدان is the prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'to those' or 'upon those' (classical be + an 'to that/those'); موبدان is the plural of موبد 'mōbed, Zoroastrian priest' and is the indirect object/goal. گوهر is the direct object 'jewel(s)' and افشاندند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they scattered/sprinkled/strew(ed)'. Word order places the indirect phrase before the object and the verb last, as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s315-g131: چو خسرو به آب مژه رخ بشست — چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; خسرو is the subject name. به here functions adverbially/instrumentally ('with/by'), so به آب مژه = 'with the water of the eyelash' (a poetic expression for tears). رخ 'face' is the object of بشست, and بشست is the simple past (3rd person) 'he washed.'
segments.s315-g132: برافشاند دینار بر زند و است — برافشاند is a past-tense verb 'scattered/shot forth' (literary form). دینار is the direct object 'dinar(s)'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; the following زند is unclear or archaic in this text (its printed senses don't match the context), but syntactically it is the noun governed by بر. و است is the copulative/continuative 'and is' or a poetic closing; the clause therefore means 'he scattered dinars upon ...'.
segments.s315-g133: به یک هفته بر پیش یزدان بدند — به یک هفته here marks a duration 'for one week' (به + numeral + زمان). بر پیش یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before God' (بر + پیش + یزدان). بدند is an archaic past 3rd-person plural form of بودن 'to be' meaning 'they were' (so: 'they (remained) for a week before God').
segments.s315-g134: مپندار کآتش پرستان بدند — مپندار is the negative imperative 'do not think / do not suppose.' ک (که) introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. آتش‌پرستان is the compound 'fire‑worshippers' (آتش + پرستان). بدند again is the past plural of بودن 'they were.' The whole clause is 'do not suppose that they were fire‑worshippers.'
segments.s315-g135: که آتش بدان گاه محراب بود — که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that.' آتش is the subject 'the fire.' بدان گاه is the temporal phrase 'at that time' (بدان = به + آن in classical spelling), and محراب بود is the predicate 'was a sanctuary' (محراب = prayer niche/sanctuary, بود = past of 'to be').
segments.s315-g136: پرستنده را دیده پرآب بود — پرستنده 'the worshipper' is topicalized, followed by را marking the definite noun (here topical emphasis). دیده here is the noun 'eye' (not a participle) and پرآب is an adjective 'full of water' (i.e. teary). بود is the past copula 'was.' Literally: 'The worshipper's eye was full of water.'
segments.s315-g65: نشستند is a third-person plural past (they sat / they took their seats); in this line the verb appears at the start for a slightly elevated word order. در + noun is the usual prepositional phrase 'in/inside', so در گلشن زرنگار = 'in the brocaded garden'; زرنگار follows the noun as an adjectival/epithet modifying گلشن (typical Persian noun + descriptive word order).
segments.s315-g66: بزرگان پرمایه is a compound noun phrase: بزرگان (the great/eminences) modified by the adjective/epithet پرمایه ('weighty', 'substantial'); با introduces the accompanying person (with + agent), so با شهریار = 'with the king'. Word order is noun–adjective then preposition + noun.
segments.s315-g67: همی here gives a continuous or iterative flavour ('kept on' / 'was saying'); گفت is the verb 'said/spoke' (the provided senses list does not include the verbal sense, hence mapped as -1); the sequence شاه آن شگفتی که دید is subject (شاه) + object clause (آن شگفتی که دید = 'that wonder which he saw'), with که introducing the relative clause.
segments.s315-g68: به دریا در is a locative expression: به + noun + در (here در = 'gate/harbour entrance') → 'at/to the sea-gate'; و links the following phrase; نامداران شنید is 'he heard from the renowned (men)' where شنید is simple past 'he heard'. The preposition به here marks the location/scene of seeing.
segments.s315-g69: ز (poetic variant of از) marks source or topic: 'from/of'; the coordination ز دریا و از گنگ دژ lists two sources ('from the sea and from the mute fortress'); یاد کرد is a light-verb construction where یاد (memory/mention) + کرد (did/made) = 'made mention of / remembered / recounted'. The verb کرد is past tense 'he did/made'.
segments.s315-g70: لب نامداران is a genitival/locative expression 'on the lips of the renowned'; پر از is an adjectival phrase 'full of X' (پر followed by از + noun); باد here is used metaphorically as 'wind' or 'breath/words' (contextual poetic sense) and کرد again is the past verb 'made/caused' — thus 'he caused the lips of the famed to be filled with wind/breath/words.'
segments.s315-g71: از آن خرمی دشت و آن شهر و راغ is a coordinate phrase introduced by از 'from/on account of' and listing three items: آن خرمی دشت ('that luxuriance/bloom of the plain'), آن شهر ('that city'), and راغ ('longing/desire'). The sequence is noun phrases joined by و; خرمی here denotes the plain's lushness/verdure.
segments.s315-g72: شمرها و پالیزها چون چراغ is a simile: two nouns (شمرها and پالیزها) are linked with و and compared using چون 'like/as' to چراغ 'a lamp/light' — 'the shams and the orchards like lamps'. The plural markers and conjunction are standard; چون introduces the likeness.
segments.s315-g1: This is a simple past clause: the light-verb construction گذر کرد (گذر + کرد) = 'passed/crossed'. The prepositional phrase به جیحون gives the thing crossed, and بر سوی بلخ is a directional phrase 'toward Balkh' (بر + سوی = toward). Word order is prepositional phrase → verb → directional complement.
segments.s315-g2: چشیده is a perfect participle (literally 'having tasted' / 'having experienced') that forms a subordinate participial clause describing the subject of the main clause. ز گیتی ('from the world') marks the source, بسی functions as an intensifier ('much' / 'greatly'), and شور و تلخ are coordinated nouns/adjectives ('tumult/ardor' and 'bitterness' — here figurative).
segments.s315-g3: The clause uses a locative phrase (به بلخ اندرون) 'in the interior of Balkh' followed by the copula بود 'was' and the nominal predicate یک ماه شاه. ماه here is a poetic epithet (= 'the moon' → 'beautiful one') modifying شاه: 'a king like the moon' or 'a beautiful king.' Word order is locative → copula → predicate.
segments.s315-g4: سر ماه is an adverbial temporal phrase 'at the start/beginning of the month' (سر = 'beginning/top'), followed by the directional phrase بر بلخ 'toward Balkh' and the verb phrase بگزید راه 'he chose the road' (گزیدن + راه = 'choose the way/road'). The verb is past tense 'chose.'
segments.s315-g5: به هر شهر is a distributive locative phrase 'in every city.' در نامور مهتری is a compact phrase: در + نامور ('in famed/renowned') with مهتری as a noun meaning 'preeminence' or 'being foremost' — together indicating 'in each famed city, in notable preeminence.' The order is emphatic and characteristic of poetic diction.
segments.s315-g6: بماندی is an older/poetic form meaning 'remained' (third-person sense 'he remained'); سرافراز is a predicate adjective 'proud/triumphant,' and با لشکری is an instrumental/comitative phrase 'with an army' specifying accompaniment. Together: 'he remained proud, with an army.'
segments.s315-g7: ببستند is a plural past verb 'they bound/tied/hung' (here used for putting up decorations). آذین is the direct object 'ornament/decoration.' The preposition به introduces the places to which the decorations were applied; بیراه و راه coordinate two types of ways ('byway and road').
segments.s315-g8: به جایی که introduces a relative clause 'to the place where...' (به جایی = 'to the place', که = relative 'that/where'), and بگذشت is the past verb 'passed by' (here 'where the king and army passed'). شاه و سپاه are the subject of that relative clause. Word order: prepositional phrase → relative clause containing verb → coordinated subject.
segments.s315-g41: 'نیا را' is the definite object ('Niyā' with the object marker را). 'چو دید' means 'when (he) saw' (چو = when + دید = saw). 'از کران' is an adverbial phrase 'from the edge/afar' (از = from, کران = edge, horizon). 'شاه نو' is the subject 'the new king' placed after the verb for poetic inversion (شاه = king, نو = new).
segments.s315-g42: برانگیخت is a past, third‑person singular verb 'roused/rose' (he/she/it sprang up). 'آن باره' is an adverbial phrase 'on that occasion / at that moment' (آن = that, باره = occasion). 'تندرو' is a predicate/epithet after the verb meaning 'the swift one' (tallies as the agent who sprang up).
segments.s315-g43: The first بر 'بر او' means 'upon/for him' (بر = upon; او = him). The repeated بر before 'نیا' indicates direction toward Niyā ('upon Niyā'); برگرفت is a past tense verb 'he lifted/took up'. آفرین here is an exclamation/noun 'afarin' = 'bravo' or 'praise' that he cried aloud. The sequence means 'he raised the cry (afarin) for him, for Niyā.'
segments.s315-g44: ستایش 'praise' is the noun head. سزای is 'desert/recompense' (here 'fitting reward/tribute'), forming a genitive relation with the following compound. جهان‌آفرین is 'Creator of the World' (a title/appositive). Together the phrase means 'praise, the fitting recompense/tribute to the Creator of the World.'
segments.s315-g45: همی گفت is a habitual/continuous past: 'he kept saying' or 'he said (repeatedly)'. بی‌تو is the prepositional phrase 'without you'. مبادا is an optative/negative particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'let there not be'. جهان is the noun 'the world' (the thing wished away). The clause reads as an exclamatory wish: 'May there be no world without you.'
segments.s315-g46: Repeated نه ... نه is the 'neither ... nor' construction. تخت بزرگی = 'throne of greatness' (تخت = throne; بزرگی = greatness, placed after the noun in Persian). تاج مهان = 'crown of the chiefs/guests' (تاج = crown; مهان = chiefs/guests), another coordinated noun phrase. The whole line lists what should not exist without the addressee.
segments.s315-g47: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'for/that'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. چون تو is a comparative phrase 'like you' (چون = like/as; تو = you) modifying the object. ندیدست is a perfective past negative 'has not seen / never saw' (here active: the sun has never seen). شاه is the object 'a king' placed after the verb by poetic inversion (i.e., 'the sun has not seen a king like you').
segments.s315-g48: Another enumerative negative with نه ... نه ...: 'نه جوشن نه اسب و نه تخت و کلاه' lists items the sun has not seen in a king like you. Each bare noun is coordinated with و 'and' (و links the last items); جوشن = cuirass/armor, اسب = horse, تخت = throne, کلاه = cap/helmet. The line emphasizes the uniqueness of the king by denying these conventional royal accoutrements.
segments.s315-g105: This clause is a verbless (stative) clause typical of Classical Persian: subject (همه رنج و سختی) + locative phrase (به پیش) + predicate expressed by the archaic copular/adverbial form اندرست. Word order puts the bulk noun phrase first, then the location. 'به پیش' means 'in front of/ before (us)' and اندرست functions as a poetic locative predicate 'lies/is at'.
segments.s315-g106: This conditional/contrasting clause begins with اگر 'if' (here best read as 'though' or 'even if') followed by چندمان 'few as we are'. دادگر is used as a description ('the just one') and یاورست is a fused predicate meaning 'is a helper' (poetic, -ست = 'is'). The clause qualifies the situation in the first half.
segments.s315-g107: چون here functions temporally ('when/when he'), so the clause reads 'When the ancestor heard...' with شنید as the past-tense verb 'heard'. از + نبیره + سخن forms a prepositional object 'of/about the scion (grandson/heir) the speech/words'. This clause is a temporal subordinate leading into the next action.
segments.s315-g108: افگند is a past tense verb 'cast/let fall/uttered' used metaphorically for 'uttered' or 'spoke'. یکی پند پیرانه is the object phrase 'one (a single) old-man's admonition' (pیرانه modifies پند). بن appears as a short poetic closing particle/word here (archaic usage); the whole means 'he uttered an old man's piece of advice.'
segments.s315-g109: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'). ما همچنین sets the speaker and manner: ما 'we' + همچنین 'thus/likewise'. بر دو اسب is the prepositional phrase 'on two horses' (بر = 'on/upon' + دو اسب). The clause introduces the reported action.
segments.s315-g110: بتازیم is a first-person-plural present/future hortatory verb 'we will gallop/charge/rush' (poetic; -یم marks 1pl). تا here is temporal/directional 'until / as far as / up to' and introduces the goal: خان آذرگشسب 'Khan Azar-Gashasp' (proper name and title).
segments.s315-g111: This is a coordinated nominal phrase plus a verb: 'سر و تن' (head and body) is the object, and بشوییم is a 1st-person-plural present/future hortatory/intentional verb 'we will wash' (poetic). با پا و دست 'with feet and hands' gives the instrument; the order is object → verb → instrument, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s315-g112: چنانچون بود is a comparative clause 'just as was...' with بود in the past tense 'was'. مرد یزدان‌پرست is the comparison standard 'the God-worshipping/pious man'. Together the clause means 'just as the pious man was' (i.e., they will behave like that pious man).
segments.s315-g73: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to/at him' (be + pronoun) marking the person causing the reaction; ماند is the past tense 'remained/was left' and follows the prepositional object here because of poetic inversion; کاووس is the subject placed after the verb (typical in epic poetry); کی is the conjunction 'when' and در شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in wonder/astonishment' specifying his state.
segments.s315-g74: ز introduces the source 'from/because of'; کردارش is a genitive 'his deeds' (کردار + possessive -ش); اندازه‌ها is the plural 'measures/extent' (here 'extent' or 'measurements'); برگرفت is a past transitive 'took up' or 'took (measure)', so together ز کردارش اندازه‌ها برگرفت = 'he took/measured the extents from his deeds' (i.e. he gauged/assessed him by his deeds).
segments.s315-g75: بدو here functions 'to/unto him' (information directed at him); گفت is used in the sense of a 'saying/declaration' (poetic usage) equivalent to 'he declared/said'; روز نو و ماه نو is a coordinated noun phrase 'a new day and a new month' with نو postpositive modifying each noun; و simply links the two nouns.
segments.s315-g76: چو introduces a comparison 'as/like'; گفتارهای نو 'new speeches/utterances' is plural with نو postpositive; و links the parallel phrase 'شاه نو' where شاه is 'king' and نو again 'new' — the whole segment gives a simile comparing the declaration to fresh words and a fresh ruler.
segments.s315-g77: نه کس is a negative subject 'no one'; چون تو is a comparative phrase 'like you' modifying شاه; اندر جهان is the locative 'in the world'; شاه is the predicate noun and دید is the past tense verb 'saw/has seen' (word order is inverted for poetic effect: Neg-subject + comparative + locative + predicate + verb).
segments.s315-g78: نه begins the second negative clause 'nor'; این داستان is the direct object 'this tale'; گوش هر کس شنید is a poetic way to say 'no ear of any person heard' — گوش 'ear' as the one that hears, هر کس 'every person' (lit. 'each person') and شنید is the past verb 'heard'.
segments.s315-g79: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame; تا here introduces a purposive clause 'so that/in order that'; بدین is the combination of the preposition ب(ه) + این meaning 'to/for this' (dataset split into ب and دین — دین in the list is a mistaken segmentation and does not carry its literal senses here); اختری 'star' (star-like) is the object; نو کنیم is the first-person-plural subjunctive/optative 'let us make/renew' (کنیم used to express purpose or exhortation).
segments.s315-g80: به here functions adverbially ('by/with' or 'in the manner of'); مردی in this construction means 'manliness' or 'manly character' (an adverbial quality: 'in manliness'); همه is the subject 'all (of us)'; یاد خسرو 'the remembrance of Khosrow' is the object phrase and کنیم repeats the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/imperative 'let us remember/perform'.
segments.s315-g9: همه بوم کشور is a fronted noun phrase meaning 'the whole land/realm' (همه modifying بوم کشور). بیاراستند is a 3rd person plural past verb (آراستن with a poetic prefix بی-) meaning 'they adorned' or 'they made beautiful.' The verb agrees with an implied plural agent who decorated the realm.
segments.s315-g10: This is a short list of things provided. می‌و and رود are nouns listed with و ('and'); رامشگران is another plural noun 'those who bring calm/comfort' and خواستند is the 3rd person plural past of خواستن meaning 'they desired/called for' (i.e. they summoned or provided). The overall sense is 'they provided wine and rivers and summoned peacemakers/comforters.'
segments.s315-g11: درم is the direct object 'dirham(s)/coin(s)' placed before the verb; ریختند is the 3rd person plural past of ریختن 'they poured/scattered (i.e. dispensed)'. از ... و زعفران links the next item (زعفران 'saffron') with a poetic prepositional marker; together it lists the valuables they distributed.
segments.s315-g12: چه here is an exclamatory intensifier 'what (a quantity of)...'. دینار و مشک are coordinated nouns 'dinar(s) and musk,' while از کران تا کران is a fixed spatial phrase 'from edge to edge' (از ... تا ... marking the extent). The line emphasizes vast quantities spread everywhere.
segments.s315-g13: به شهر اندرون is a locative phrase 'to/into the inner part of the city' (به + noun + اندرون). هر که introduces a free relative 'whoever/anyone who' and درویش بود is the predicate 'was a dervish/poor person' with بود as the past copula.
segments.s315-g14: وگر = 'but if' (or 'and if'); سازش is the subject/noun meaning 'settlement/adjustment/recompense' here (i.e. any relief or arrangement). از marks the source 'from,' کوشش خویش = 'his own effort' where خوی + enclitic ش form 'his own,' and بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause says any accommodation/benefit came from the person's own exertion.
segments.s315-g15: This clause says who received money and from what source. درم is the coin 'a dirham'; داد is the 3rd person singular past 'he gave'; مر is a dative/indirect-object form (here marking the recipient(s) in a generic/poetic way); هر یکی را literally 'each one (را marks the object)' and ز گنج 'from the treasure' gives the source. Together: 'He gave each one a dirham from the treasury.'
segments.s315-g16: پراگنده is an adjective 'scattered' (used predicatively); شد is the past of شدن 'became' (resultative). بدره is a poetic noun 'full moon' used as a simile for brilliance, and the following پنجاه و پنج is the numeral 'fifty‑five' (و simply coordinates parts of the numeral). The phrase means the dispersion/scene shone or spread like fifty‑five full moons.
segments.s315-g17: 'سر هفته' is an adverbial temporal phrase meaning 'at the start/beginning of the week' (literal 'head of the week'). The clitic را after that phrase works as a topical/emphatic marker here. کرد is simple past 'did/made' and with آهنگ (intention/aim) forms the idiom 'کرد آهنگِ X' = 'set his course/turned his intent to X'; ری names the place (Ray). Word order is topical-time + را, then verb + its object (آهنگِ ری).
segments.s315-g18: سوی پارس = 'toward Pars/Persia' (سوی = toward, پارس = Persia). نزدیک here is an adverb/adjectival modifier 'near' and attaches to the following name. کاووس is the king's name; کی in this line is the royal epithet 'Kay' used after the name because of poetic inversion (so here it functions as a title, not the interrogative). The whole phrase locates the direction/place: 'toward Pars, near Kay Kavus.'
segments.s315-g19: دو هفته is a duration phrase 'two weeks' placed before the verb. به ری indicates the place/recipient ('to/at Ray'). نیز 'also' modifies the action. بخشید here means 'granted/allocated' (classical use meaning 'spent/allowed' time or bestowed), in past tense. و خورد follows as a simple coordinated verb 'and ate' (i.e., feasted). The sequence means he remained/spent/gave two weeks at Ray and feasted.
segments.s315-g20: سیم هفته is an ordinal-time expression 'the third week' (سیم = third). آهنگ ... کرد repeats the idiom 'made/set his course for …' with بغداد (Baghdad) as the destination and کرد as past 'did/made'. The whole means 'in the third week he set his course for Baghdad.'
segments.s315-g21: هیونان (plural) 'beasts/creatures' is the (direct) object of فرستاد 'sent' (past). چندی placed after the verb means 'some/several' (quantifier). ز ری is a poetic ablative 'from Ray'. The basic order is object + verb + quantifier + source: 'He sent several beasts from Ray.'
segments.s315-g22: به نزدیک = 'to the vicinity/near' (به + نزدیک). کاووس is the king's name and فرخنده‌پی is an epithet ('fortunate-footed') attached to him; epithets often follow the name in poetic diction. Together this phrase indicates the destination: 'to the vicinity of Kay Kavus, the Fortunate‑footed.'
segments.s315-g23: دل پیر = 'the old man's heart' (دل 'heart', پیر 'old'). زان 'from that' is an ablative/causal particle ('from that [news]'). آگهی 'news/information' is the cause, تازه شد is the compound 'became fresh/was refreshed' (تازه = new/fresh + شد = became), so the clause reads 'the old man's heart was renewed by that news.'
segments.s315-g24: تو is the 2nd‑person subject 'you'. گفتی is the past 'you said' (the listed senses in the data do not include the verb sense, so no listed sense matches exactly). که introduces reported speech ('that'). بر here functions as a prepositional/relational particle 'into/on' or 'upon' used with اندازه 'measure/degree' to mean 'into another measure/degree', and شد is past 'became/was made.' The clause therefore reports: 'you said that it had been made/was become of another measure.'
segments.s315-g49: This is a prepositional range: ز ... تا ... reached. ز (z) + name marks the start of the span (“from Jamshid”), تا introduces the end point (“until/as far as Afrīdūn”), and رسید is a 3rd-person past verb meaning “reached/extended to.” Put together the clause says the chronological or genealogical span extends from Jamshid to Afrīdūn.
segments.s315-g50: سپهر و زمین is the compound subject (“heaven and earth”). چون تو is a simile phrase placed before the predicate meaning “like you.” شاهی here names the kind/office of king (kingship/royal stature) and ندید is the past negative of دیدن (“did not see”). Word order: subject → simile phrase → object/predicate.
segments.s315-g51: نه ... کسی ... رنج برد is a negative-existential phrase: نه ... کسی = “no one.” زین سان is an idiom meaning “in this manner/so” (here poetic); رنج برد is the verbal expression “to bear/endure suffering” where برد is part of the compound بردن. از + مهان means “from/of the great/visitors” (here ‘the great’ or ‘magnates’ causing the suffering).
segments.s315-g52: نه دید = “he did not see” (negative past). آشکارا نهان جهان strings together the opposites “the manifest (آشکارا) and the hidden (نهان) of the world (جهان),” i.e. ‘neither the open nor the secret things of the world.’ The verb (دید) governs both nouns as two coordinated objects/qualities.
segments.s315-g53: که at the start introduces a wish/exclamation here (“may …”). روشن جهان = “the bright/enlightened world.” بر تو marks the beneficiary/target (“upon you/for you”). فرخنده باد is an optative formula: فرخنده = “auspicious/fortunate,” باد = “may it be.” Together: “May the bright world be auspicious to you.”
segments.s315-g54: دل و جان = “heart and life/soul.” بدخواه تو is a genitive/compound meaning “your ill-wisher/enemy” (the one hostile to you). کنده is a past participle meaning “uprooted/removed,” and باد again is the optative particle “may (it be).” The construction expresses a wish that the enemy’s heart and life be uprooted (i.e. ruined).
segments.s315-g55: This begins with the name Sīyāvoš (vocative or topical). گرش روز = “the turn/rotation (گرش) of the day (روز),” i.e. the day of return or the time of his fortune. باز is the adverb “again.” آمدی is a past/poetic form of آمدن here meaning “comes/arrives” (classical/poetic verb forms sometimes show different endings); syntactically this clause sets up a conditional/time frame: “if/when his day returns again…”
segments.s315-g56: به فر تو = “to/into your glory/presence” (به = to/for; فر = royal glory). او را is the topicalized/marked pronoun “him” (with را as the object/topic marker). نیاز آمدی is an idiomatic sequence meaning “came in supplication” or “came beseeching” (نیاز = plea/supplication; آمدی = came). The whole clause: “he will come to your glory in supplication.”
